svg.title: Matrix Chain Multiplication — full step-by-step walkthrough with DP tables
svg.desc: Explains the dynamic programming recurrence with worked examples for each chain length, compressing repeated identical procedures, showing the full m and s DP tables, and ending with the traceback to the optimal parenthesization.

g.text: Matrix Chain Multiplication — step by step
Step 1 — Setup: what are we solving?

svg.text: We want the cheapest order to multiply 4 matrices together.
dp[i][j] = minimum scalar multiplications to multiply matrices i through j.
Base case: dp[i][i] = 0 (a single matrix needs no multiplication).

g.text: Step 2 — Chain length l=2: pairs of adjacent matrices

svg.text: For a chain of just 2 matrices, there's only ONE way to multiply them —
no choice to make. Cost = rows(first) × shared_dim × cols(second).
We compute this for every adjacent pair: (A₁,A₂), (A₂,A₃), (A₃,A₄).

g.text: Step 5 — Chain length l=3: now there's a CHOICE

svg.text: For 3+ matrices, there are multiple ways to group them — we must try
every split point k and keep the cheapest. m[i][j] = min over k of:
m[i][k] + m[k+1][j] + (cost of multiplying the two resulting blocks).
Winner: k=2 is cheaper → m[1][3]=4500, remember split point s[1][3]=2
Step 6 (same procedure as Step 5 — try every k, keep cheapest):
Winner: k=2 is cheaper → m[2][4]=9000, split point s[2][4]=2

g.text: Step 7 — Chain length l=4: the full chain (final answer)

svg.text: Same procedure again, but now we're combining ALL 4 matrices. We try
every split point using the smaller results we already computed —
this reuse of subproblems is exactly why dynamic programming is fast.
Winner: k=2 is cheapest → m[1][4]=8500, split point s[1][4]=2

g.text: DP Tables — the full picture at once

svg.text: Everything above was building these two tables, cell by cell. The m
table stores minimum costs; the s table stores which split achieved
them — s is what the traceback below actually walks through.

g.text: Step 8 — Traceback: reconstruct the actual grouping

svg.text: We walk the split points backward: s[1][4]=2 splits into (A₁A₂)(A₃A₄).
Then s[1][2]=1 means A₁A₂ needs no further split — it's just (A₁A₂).
s[3][4]=3 means A₃A₄ needs no further split — it's just (A₃A₄).
